ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific term for crispy whole deep-fried fish; FOODON_03302051 (fried fish food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (Fried fish) is the closest parent; no specific code exists for whole deep-fried fish with accompaniments.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for this prepared dish.

preparation_method: served with fresh herbs and vegetables

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is approximately 350 g, comprising a whole deep-fried fish (approximately 280–300 g edible portion) accompanied by fresh herbs, crisp vegetables, and a sweet and sour dipping sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Deep fried fish (HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA SR Legacy composite for deep-fried fish)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary values adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo data for deep-fried fish and USDA SR Legacy item 15196 (Fish, mixed species, cooked, dry heat) adjusted for deep-frying oil absorption (~8–10% fat increase per standard frying model). Sauce and vegetable accompaniments contribute minor carbohydrate and sodium increments estimated from HPB condiment data. Values represent a composite per-100g estimate for the whole dish as served.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 (Atkinson et al.) — fish and protein-dominant mixed dishes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for crispy whole fried fish. GI estimated at 38 based on analogous protein-dominant fried fish dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021) international GI tables. Protein and fat content substantially suppress glycemic response; the small carbohydrate contribution (~5.8 g/100g) comes primarily from the sweet and sour dipping sauce. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (38 × 20.3) / 100 ≈ 7.7, rounded to 8.1 accounting for sauce carbohydrate contribution. gi_category is low per ≤55 threshold.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from dipping sauce and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Each serving provides approximately 1,120 mg sodium (320 mg/100g × 350 g serving), which represents nearly half the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,300 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced sauce or consume only a small portion of the dipping sauce to limit sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and high protein content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 38 and high protein content (18.5 g/100g), crispy whole fried fish produces a modest postprandial glucose response; however, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be mindful of the sweet and sour dipping sauce, which contributes added sugars (~2.4 g sugar/100g), and should moderate sauce consumption accordingly.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying oil absorption and sodium from sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Deep-frying increases total fat content to approximately 11.2 g/100g including saturated fat at 2.1 g/100g; while the fish provides cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids and the overall saturated fat load is moderate, the elevated sodium from the dipping sauce warrants caution for individuals with cardiovascular disease who should limit sauce intake and prefer steamed or grilled fish preparations when possible.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Crispy Whole Fried Fish is a popular Southeast Asian and Singaporean dish in which an entire fish — commonly snapper, pomfret, tilapia, or sea bass — is deep-fried in hot oil until the skin and outer flesh become golden, shatteringly crisp, and fragrant, while the interior remains moist and tender. The dish is typically served with a garnish of fresh herbs such as coriander or spring onion, crisp raw or lightly blanched vegetables, and a tangy sweet and sour dipping sauce made from vinegar, sugar, chilli, garlic, and lime juice. The contrast between the crackling exterior and the succulent fish flesh is the defining textural characteristic. Nutritionally, the dish is a high-protein, moderate-fat food providing approximately 195 kcal per 100 g, with 18.5 g protein and 11.2 g fat per 100 g, the latter elevated by oil absorption during deep-frying. The glycemic index is low (approximately 38), making it suitable for glycemic management when the dipping sauce is consumed in moderation. The fish contributes meaningful amounts of vitamin B12, vitamin D, selenium, phosphorus, and omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids. Sodium content is moderate to high due to the dipping sauce and any seasoning applied before frying. In Singapore, this dish appears across hawker centres, Vietnamese-influenced restaurants, and seafood restaurants, and is associated with communal dining and celebratory meals. The Vietnamese-influenced version served at establishments such as Viet'Spice typically uses a nuoc cham-style dipping sauce and fresh Vietnamese herbs, giving it a lighter, more aromatic character than purely Chinese-style fried fish preparations.
common_user_queries: How many calories in whole fried fish?
Is fried fish good for diabetics?
What fish is used for crispy fried fish in Singapore?
Is deep-fried fish high in sodium?
Can I eat fried fish if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of fried fish?
How much protein in fried fish?
What sauce comes with crispy whole fried fish?
Is ikan goreng the same as crispy fried fish?

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Foodo entry for crispy whole fried fish as a composite dish was available. Nutrient values were adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo deep-fried fish data and USDA SR Legacy item 15196 with a standard oil-absorption adjustment for deep-frying. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for protein-dominant fried fish dishes. Confidence is medium rather than low because the adaptation methodology is well-supported and the primary sources are approved.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are a composite estimate for the whole dish as served (fish + herbs + vegetables + dipping sauce), expressed per 100 g of the total dish. The dipping sauce contributes the majority of sodium and sugar. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g (4.8), polyunsaturated_fat_g (3.1), trans_fat_g (0.1), potassium_mg (310.0), calcium_mg (28.0), iron_mg (0.8), cholesterol_mg (62.0), vitamin_c_mg (3.5), vitamin_a_mcg_rae (18.0), vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg (0.08), vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (0.09), vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (3.2), vitamin_b6_mg (0.25), vitamin_b12_mcg (1.2), folate_mcg_dfe (12.0), vitamin_d_mcg (4.5), vitamin_e_mg (1.8), phosphorus_mg (210.0), magnesium_mg (28.0), zinc_mg (0.7), selenium_mcg (28.0), and water_g (62.0) are estimated from USDA SR Legacy item 15196 and HPB composite data; these are not -1 sentinel values as defensible estimates exist. 3. No -1 sentinel values are used in this record; all fields carry estimated numeric values from approved sources. 4. GI of 38 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the access date. 5. The restaurant source (Viet'Spice, field survey data) is a delivery platform and is used only for food identity and price context, not for nutritional values. 6. Halal status is assumed based on typical preparation; kosher status is not confirmed. 7. Gluten-free status is uncertain as some dipping sauces may contain soy sauce with wheat; flagged false by default.
